---
title: "STSCI 5740 Project Report"
author: "Kelvin Chi (kc2346), Nina Wang (nw348), Tianle Pang (tp439) and Xibei Xie (xx375)"
format:
  pdf:
    toc: false
    number-sections: true
    number-offset: [0]
    top-level-division: section
    fontsize: 12pt
    papersize: a4
    linestretch: 1.5
    fig-align: center
    geometry:
      - margin=0.5in
      - heightrounded
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{etoolbox}
        \usepackage{setspace}
        \usepackage{caption}
        \usepackage{array}
        \DeclareCaptionFont{eightpt}{\fontsize{8}{9.6}\selectfont}
        \newcommand{\ninept}{\fontsize{9}{10.8}\selectfont}
        \captionsetup[figure]{font=eightpt}
        \setlength{\tabcolsep}{2pt}
        \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{0.92}
        \setlength{\aboverulesep}{0pt}
        \setlength{\belowrulesep}{0pt}
        \setlength{\extrarowheight}{0pt}
        \setlength{\LTleft}{0pt}
        \setlength{\LTright}{0pt}
        \setkeys{Gin}{width=0.40\linewidth,keepaspectratio=true}
        \AtBeginEnvironment{figure}{\centering}
        \AtBeginEnvironment{table}{\ninept\setstretch{1.0}}
        \AtBeginEnvironment{longtable}{\ninept\setstretch{1.0}}
---

# Introduction

This project applies machine learning methods to predict hospital readmission outcomes among patients with diabetes using the **Diabetes 130-US hospitals dataset (1999-2008)** from the **UCI Machine Learning Repository**. The dataset contains inpatient encounter records from 130 U.S. hospitals and includes demographic variables, admission characteristics, diagnoses, laboratory information, medication usage, and prior healthcare utilization measures.

The objective is to build and compare classification models for readmission prediction. Methods considered in this project include regularized logistic regression, general additive models, random forest, and gradient boosting. Model performance is compared to assess predictive accuracy and identify useful patterns in the data.

Project code base are available on GitHub: [stsci5740-project](https://github.com/kelvinchi02/stsci5740-project).

# Data Cleaning

This section summarizes the preprocessing steps used to transform the raw UCI Diabetes dataset into a modeling-ready patient-level dataset.

## Patient-Level Deduplication

To avoid repeated observations from the same patient, only the first encounter for each unique `patient_nbr` was retained. - Multiple encounters from the same patient may introduce dependence across rows. - Dataset size was reduced from **101,766** to **71,518** observations.

## Sparse Feature Removal

Variables with substantial missingness or limited modeling value were removed: - `weight` (96.01% missing) - `medical_specialty` (48.21% missing) - `payer_code` (43.41% missing) These variables contained a large proportion of missing values and would require extensive imputation with limited expected benefit.

## Missing and Invalid Value Treatment

Missing or ambiguous values were handled using variable-specific rules: - `race`: missing values grouped as `Other` - `max_glu_serum`, `A1Cresult`: `None` retained as a valid category - `gender`: `Unknown/Invalid` rows removed - `diag_1`: missing rows removed (11 observations) - `diag_2`, `diag_3`: missing values recoded as `None` This approach preserved sample size while maintaining consistency across predictors.

## Diagnosis Code Consolidation

ICD-9 diagnosis codes in `diag_1`, `diag_2`, and `diag_3` were grouped into broader clinical categories such as Circulatory, Respiratory, Digestive, Diabetes based on the first three digits of the code. This reduced the number of unique diagnosis categories from over 700 to a manageable set of 10 clinically meaningful groups. This reduced high-cardinality categorical levels and improved interpretability.

## Metadata Label Mapping

Administrative ID variables were converted into descriptive categorical labels based on provided mapping. This prevents models from incorrectly treating these coded fields as numeric quantities.

## Target Variable Creation

The original `readmitted` variable had three levels: `<30`, `>30`, and `NO`. For modeling purposes, a binary target variable `readmitted_binary` was created: - `readmitted_binary = 1` if `readmitted` = `<30` (indicating readmission within 30 days) - `readmitted_binary = 0` if `readmitted` = `>30` or `NO` (indicating no readmission within 30 days)

## Final Output

After cleaning and preprocessing, the final dataset contained: - **71,504 observations** - **47 variables** This cleaned dataset was used for exploratory analysis and predictive modeling.

# Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

This section summarizes the main empirical patterns observed in the cleaned patient-level dataset. The purpose of EDA is to understand variable distributions, identify potential data quality issues.

## Outcome Distribution

The binary readmission target is strongly imbalanced: - `readmitted_binary = 0` (not readmitted within 30 days): **65,213** - `readmitted_binary = 1` (readmitted within 30 days): **6,291** This corresponds to a minority class rate of approximately **8.8%**. Because the outcome variable is imbalanced, model evaluation focused primarily on ROC AUC and confusion-matrix-based classification summaries. Additional metrics such as precision, recall, and F1-score can be derived to assess minority-class detection performance.

## Categorical Variable Patterns

Faceted count plots were used to examine demographic, diagnosis, administrative, and medication variables.

Main observations: - `race` is dominated by Caucasian patients, followed by African American patients. - Age is concentrated in older groups, particularly ages **60-70**. - Several administrative categories contain uneven level frequencies. - Most medication indicators are dominated by the `No` category. - The positive readmission class remains a minority across nearly all factor levels. These patterns indicate class imbalance not only in the target variable, but also uneven representation across several predictors.

## Numerical Variable Distributions

Density plots were created for numerical predictors:

![Numerical predictor density plots by readmission status.](outputs/eda_numeric_distributions.png)

Main observations: - Most utilization variables are right-skewed. - Many count variables contain mass near zero with long upper tails. - Readmitted patients tend to exhibit slightly heavier tails in prior utilization measures. - Considerable overlap remains between outcome groups, suggesting nonlinear boundaries may be useful.

## Correlation Structure

A correlation heatmap was used to assess dependence among numerical predictors.

![Correlation heatmap for numerical predictors.](outputs/correlation_heatmap.png)

Main observations: - Most pairwise correlations are low to moderate. - Stronger relationships appear among healthcare utilization variables including `time_in_hospital`, `num_medications`,`num_procedures`,`num_lab_procedures` - No severe multicollinearity pattern is evident. This supports the use of both linear and regularized models without major instability concerns.

# Linear Modeling

The first modeling stage focused on interpretable linear classification methods. Given the large number of categorical predictors after encoding, regularized logistic regression was prioritized over subset-selection procedures. Providing generally more stable, scalable, and better suited for higher-dimensional encoded feature spaces. - **Lasso Logistic Regression** ($L_1$ penalty) was used for coefficient shrinkage and automatic variable selection. - **Ridge Logistic Regression** ($L_2$ penalty) was used to reduce coefficient variance and stabilize estimates under correlated predictors. - Lasso can shrink some coefficients exactly to zero, producing a sparse model. - Ridge retains all predictors while shrinking coefficient magnitudes. - Both models use cross-validation to select the tuning parameter $\lambda$.

## Preprocessing and Train-Test Split

1.  **Train-Test Split:** The cleaned dataset was split into training and testing sets using a stratified **80/20 split** by `readmitted_binary`.
2.  **Feature Encoding:** Categorical predictors were converted to one-hot dummy variables.
3.  **Scaling:** Numeric predictors were standardized for regularized modeling.
4.  **Applied to Test Set via Workflow:** The trained recipe was applied to the held-out test set through the same workflow.

## Regularized Logistic Regression (LR)

An elastic net logistic regression model was tuned using cross-validation, with **ROC AUC** as the optimization metric. The search varied: - `penalty` = regularization strength (\lambda) - `mixture` = penalty type parameter (0 for Ridge, 1 for Lasso) (\alpha)

### Best Model Parameters

The best-performing model from cross-validation was:

| Penalty ((\lambda)) | Mixture ((\alpha)) | Selected Model | Config |
|------------------:|------------------:|------------------|------------------|
| 0.0774 | 0 | Ridge Logistic Regression | `pre0_mod17_post0` |

The figure below shows the tuning search over penalty values and model types.

![Cross-validation tuning surface for logistic penalty search.](outputs/logistic_lambda_search.png)

The result indicates that shrinking all coefficients performed better than sparse variable selection. This suggests predictive information is distributed across many variables rather than concentrated in only a few predictors.

### Important Predictors

The table below shows the most meaningful predictors from the selected Ridge logistic regression model. Positive coefficients increase the log-odds of readmission, while negative coefficients decrease it.

| Predictor | Coefficient | Interpretation |
|----------------------|---------------------------:|----------------------|
| `number_inpatient` | +0.1360 | Prior inpatient admissions are the strongest predictor of future readmission |
| Discharged / transferred to another rehab facility | +0.1200 | Complex discharge pathways linked to elevated risk |
| Discharged to home | -0.0889 | Returning home associated with lower readmission risk |
| Expired | -0.0825 | Negative coefficient due to coding structure / terminal outcome |
| Discharged to SNF | +0.0675 | Skilled nursing discharge associated with higher risk |
| `time_in_hospital` | +0.0425 | Longer stays may indicate greater illness severity |
| `number_diagnoses` | +0.0365 | More diagnoses imply higher comorbidity burden |
| `diag_1_Respiratory` | -0.0361 | Respiratory primary diagnosis showed lower relative risk vs baseline group |
| Age 50-60 | -0.0345 | Slightly lower risk relative to reference age category |
| `diabetesMed_Yes` | +0.0332 | Medication usage may proxy greater disease severity |

The strongest predictors are primarily related to: - prior healthcare utilization (`number_inpatient`, `number_emergency`) - discharge disposition - disease burden (`number_diagnoses`) - hospital stay intensity (`time_in_hospital`) - age and diagnosis categories This suggests readmission risk is driven more by patient complexity and prior utilization history than by any single demographic variable.

### ROC Curve

The ROC curve below shows test-set predictive discrimination for the selected Ridge logistic regression model.

![Test ROC curve for the final ridge logistic regression model.](outputs/lr_final_roc_curve.png)

### Performance Interpretation

The final ridge logistic regression model achieved a **test ROC AUC of 0.648**, indicating meaningful predictive power above random guessing (AUC = 0.5). This confirms that the cleaned predictors contain useful signal for readmission prediction, even under a linear modeling framework.

Key findings: - **Ridge and Lasso performed very similarly overall**, indicating that both regularization approaches were effective for this dataset. - **Ridge outperformed Lasso by a small margin** during cross-validation and was therefore selected as the final linear model. - The small performance gap suggests that predictive information is spread across many variables, with both sparse and shrinkage-based methods capturing similar structure. - Retaining all predictors with coefficient shrinkage provided a slight advantage over variable selection alone.

### Conclusion

Among linear models, **Ridge logistic regression** was the best-performing regularized classifier and was selected as the final logistic benchmark for subsequent model comparisons.

## Generalized Additive Models (GAM)

To relax the linearity assumption imposed by logistic regression, a **Generalized Additive Model (GAM)** was fitted on the same train/test split. GAMs extend logistic regression by allowing selected numerical predictors to have smooth nonlinear effects while preserving an additive and interpretable structure.

The fitted model uses a **binomial family** with **logit link**, making it directly comparable to the earlier logistic regression models.

### Model Specification and Complexity

The GAM included smooth spline terms for continuous predictors and standard factor effects for categorical predictors. Predictor choices were based on logistic-model variable performance and clinical interpretability.

| Smoothed Numerical Predictor |  EDF | Linear/Categorical Predictor |
|------------------------------|-----:|------------------------------|
| `time_in_hospital`           | 3.30 | `race`                       |
| `num_lab_procedures`         | 2.18 | `gender`                     |
| `num_medications`            | 1.04 | `age`                        |
| `number_diagnoses`           | 2.39 | `diabetesMed`                |
| `number_inpatient`           | 2.65 | `insulin`                    |
| `number_emergency`           | 2.10 | `discharge_disposition_id`   |

All smoothed terms were modeled with basis size **k = 5**. Total effective degrees of freedom: **39.65**. UBRE score: **-0.4338**.

![Estimated smooth effects from the fitted GAM model.](outputs/gam_smooth_plots.png)

### Interpretation

The GAM achieved a **test ROC AUC of 0.648**, indicating meaningful predictive power above random guessing.

Key variable insights: - `number_inpatient` showed the strongest increasing nonlinear effect. - `number_emergency` displayed a curved relationship. - Several predictors such as `num_medications` were nearly linear.

### Conclusion

The GAM provided an interpretable nonlinear extension of logistic regression and achieved competitive performance (**AUC = 0.648**). However, the similarity to regularized logistic models suggests that more complex interactions or ensemble methods may be needed for larger predictive gains.

# Nonlinear Modeling

After evaluating linear and additive models, the next stage focused on nonlinear machine learning methods that can capture complex interactions and nonlinearities without requiring explicit specification.

## Random Forest (RF)

To model nonlinear relationships and interaction effects, a **Random Forest** classifier was fitted. This approach can capture complex predictor interactions without requiring manual feature engineering.

### Model Tuning

The Random Forest model was tuned using cross-validation over: - `mtry` = number of predictors sampled at each split - `min_n` = minimum node size The number of trees was fixed at **500**, and **PR AUC** was used as the primary tuning metric in the final imbalance-aware pass.

### Interpretation of Metrics

Under the default class decision rule on the held-out test set, RF produced:

- **PR AUC = 0.179**
- **ROC AUC = 0.650**
- **Balanced Accuracy = 0.500**
- **Recall (Yes class) = 0.000**
- **Specificity (Yes class) = 1.000**

This indicates that while RF retained useful ranking ability (AUC above 0.5), default classification behavior did not identify positive readmissions. Therefore, threshold selection is a critical downstream decision.

### Variable Importance

The top predictors based on **Gini impurity importance** are shown below.

![Random Forest variable importance ranked by Gini importance.](outputs/rf_variable_importance.png)

These results suggest utilization intensity, treatment burden, and discharge outcomes are key drivers of readmission risk.

### Conclusion

Random Forest provided competitive ranking performance (**ROC AUC = 0.650**) and clear variable-importance signals, but required post-hoc threshold tuning for clinically useful detection.

## Gradient Boosted Trees (XGBoost)

To further improve predictive performance, an **Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)** classifier was fitted using the same train/test split.

This method is effective for structured tabular data because it captures nonlinear effects, predictor interactions, and complex decision boundaries while extracting robust signal from mixed feature types.

### Model Tuning

The model was tuned using cross-validation with racing methods over the following hyperparameters: - `tree_depth` - `min_n` - `loss_reduction` - `learn_rate` - `sample_size` The number of trees was fixed at **1000**, with **early stopping rounds = 10** to reduce overfitting.

ROC AUC was used as the primary selection metric.

### Interpretation of Metrics

Under the default class decision rule on the held-out test set, XGBoost produced:

- **ROC AUC = 0.651**
- **PR AUC = 0.181**
- **Balanced Accuracy = 0.500**
- **Recall (Yes class) = 0.000**
- **Specificity (Yes class) = 1.000**

As with RF, the model showed useful ranking performance but default threshold behavior was too conservative for positive-case detection.

### Variable Importance

The top predictors based on gain importance are shown below.

![XGBoost variable importance ranked by gain.](outputs/xgb_variable_importance.png)

These results reinforce earlier findings that prior utilization history, treatment intensity, and discharge pathways are the strongest signals of readmission risk.

### Conclusion

XGBoost achieved the strongest ranking performance among evaluated models and became the leading candidate for objective-driven threshold tuning.

# Final Model Comparison

## Test Set Performance

| Model                     |   ROC AUC |    PR AUC |
|---------------------------|----------:|----------:|
| Ridge Logistic Regression |     0.648 |     0.167 |
| GAM                       |     0.648 |     0.166 |
| Random Forest             |     0.650 |     0.179 |
| XGBoost                   | **0.651** | **0.181** |

Because the dataset is strongly imbalanced, ranking metrics are the primary comparison basis.

- **XGBoost achieved the best overall discrimination** in both ROC AUC and PR AUC among nonlinear models.
- **Random Forest performed similarly in ROC AUC**, with slightly lower PR AUC.
- **Ridge Logistic Regression and GAM remained competitive** in ROC AUC but trailed the tree-based models on PR AUC.

## Threshold-Based Classification Behavior

Default class predictions were not clinically usable for minority-case detection.

| Metric | Ridge Logistic Regression | GAM | Random Forest | XGBoost |
|----|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Predicted positive cases (default threshold) | 2 | 6 | 0 | 0 |

This confirms that probability ranking ability is more meaningful than raw default-threshold class labels in this setting.

# Threshold Optimization

To make thresholding deployment-oriented, thresholds were selected on **CV predictions from training data** under an explicit clinical objective, then evaluated once on the held-out test set.

To prevent leakage, no held-out test labels were used during threshold selection.

Clinical objective used in this project:

- Min precision: **0.15**
- Max alert rate: **0.30**
- Among feasible thresholds, maximize recall.

This supports a care-management screening workflow where missing high-risk patients is costly, while outreach capacity is limited.

## Optimization Curves

The figure below shows threshold tradeoffs used for objective-based selection.

![Clinical objective threshold tradeoff curves across models.](outputs/threshold_clinical_objective_plots.png)

Selected CV thresholds:

| Model         | Threshold | Selection Mode    |
|---------------|----------:|-------------------|
| Lasso         |     0.097 | meets constraints |
| Random Forest |     0.094 | meets constraints |
| XGBoost       |     0.011 | meets constraints |
| GAM           |     0.100 | meets constraints |

## Tuned Confusion Matrices

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### Ridge/Lasso (t = 0.097)

| Prediction | True No | True Yes |
|------------|--------:|---------:|
| No         |   9,872 |      739 |
| Yes        |   3,121 |      569 |
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### RF (t = 0.094)

| Prediction | True No | True Yes |
|------------|--------:|---------:|
| No         |   8,533 |      600 |
| Yes        |   4,460 |      708 |
:::
:::::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
#### XGB (t = 0.011)

| Prediction | True No | True Yes |
|------------|--------:|---------:|
| No         |   9,745 |      710 |
| Yes        |   3,248 |      598 |
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
#### GAM (t = 0.100)

| Prediction | True No | True Yes |
|------------|--------:|---------:|
| No         |   9,963 |      745 |
| Yes        |   3,030 |      563 |
:::
:::::

## Interpretation

Held-out test objective check:

| Model | Precision | Recall | Specificity | F1 | Balanced Accuracy | Alert Rate | Meets Objective |
|---------|--------:|--------:|--------:|--------:|--------:|--------:|---------|
| Lasso | 0.154 | 0.435 | 0.760 | 0.228 | 0.597 | 0.258 | Yes |
| Random Forest | 0.137 | 0.541 | 0.657 | 0.219 | 0.599 | 0.361 | No |
| XGBoost | 0.155 | 0.457 | 0.750 | 0.232 | 0.604 | 0.269 | Yes |
| GAM | 0.157 | 0.430 | 0.767 | 0.230 | 0.599 | 0.251 | Yes |

Interpretation:

- **XGBoost best satisfied the practical objective**, providing the highest recall among objective-feasible models.
- **Random Forest had higher recall but exceeded outreach capacity and failed the precision floor**, so it is not operationally feasible under this objective.
- **Lasso and GAM met constraints** with slightly lower recall than XGBoost.

## Conclusion

Threshold selection had a larger practical effect than algorithm changes alone. Under a clinically constrained objective, **XGBoost (threshold = 0.011)** provided the best balance between detection and operational capacity.

# Final Conclusion

This project compared linear, additive, and tree-based models for 30-day readmission risk and found modest but consistent discrimination (ROC AUC roughly **0.648 to 0.651**).

Key findings:

- **Best ranking model:** XGBoost (ROC AUC 0.651, PR AUC 0.181).
- **Default class thresholds were not clinically useful** in this imbalanced setting.
- **Leakage-safe threshold workflow:** thresholds were chosen using CV predictions from training data and only then applied to the held-out test set.
- **Objective-driven thresholding was essential** for deployment realism.

Using a practical care-management objective (minimum precision 0.15, maximum alert rate 0.30), **XGBoost** was selected as the operational recommendation. On held-out test data at threshold **0.011**, it achieved:

- Precision: **0.155**
- Recall: **0.457**
- Specificity: **0.750**
- F1: **0.232**
- Balanced Accuracy: **0.604**
- Alert Rate: **0.269**

Overall conclusion: for this dataset, model choice matters, but **threshold policy under clinical constraints matters at least as much** for practical utility.
